questions.question: Explain the difference between true sharing and false sharing with an example in OpenMP.
keyPoints: True sharing: multiple threads access same memory location—actual data dependency exists
False sharing: threads access different variables that happen to share same cache line
Example: In parallel loop updating arr[i], if thread 0 writes arr[0] and thread 1 writes arr[1], they may share cache line causing false sharing
Performance impact: False sharing causes severe performance degradation despite no logical data dependency

questions.question: Describe the four states of the MESI cache coherence protocol and explain what each state means.
keyPoints: Modified: Cache line is exclusive to this cache and has been modified (dirty) - must write back
Exclusive: Cache line is exclusive to this cache but clean (same as memory)
Shared: Cache line is present in multiple caches and is clean
Invalid: Cache line is not present in this cache or contains stale data
State transitions occur on reads, writes, and bus transactions

questions.question: How can cache line padding help eliminate false sharing? Show a C structure example.
keyPoints: Padding adds dummy bytes between variables to place them on different cache lines
Typical cache line is 64 bytes on modern processors
Example struct: struct padded_counter { long counter; char pad[56]; }; // 56 = 64 - 8
Each counter now occupies its own cache line, preventing invalidation ping-pong
Alignment directives like __attribute__((aligned(64))) achieve similar effect

questions.question: Why does false sharing worsen as the number of processor cores increases?
keyPoints: More cores means more threads potentially accessing the same cache lines
Cache coherence traffic scales poorly (O(n) or worse for snooping protocols)
Each additional thread increases probability of cache line contention
On many-core systems, coherence bandwidth becomes bottleneck
Hardware performance counters show increasing invalidation rates with core count

questions.question: How would you use OpenMP reduction clause to avoid false sharing in a parallel summation?
keyPoints: Without reduction: shared variable causes false sharing as each thread updates it
Reduction clause creates private copy for each thread, eliminates shared updates during loop
At loop end, private copies are combined into final result
Example: #pragma omp parallel for reduction(+:sum)
This is more efficient than critical sections or atomic operations which serialize access

questions.question: What is write-invalidate coherence protocol and how does it cause false sharing overhead?
keyPoints: Write-invalidate: Before writing to a cache line in shared state, core invalidates copies in other caches
Other cores must reload the cache line on their next access
False sharing occurs when different variables share same line—each write invalidates others' copies
Result: excessive memory traffic as cache lines bounce between cores
Even though threads access logically independent data, hardware treats entire line atomically

questions.question: Consider a parallel histogram computation where each thread counts occurrences in its portion of data. The code uses an array of bins shared among threads with each thread incrementing its assigned bin. Explain how false sharing can occur and propose two solutions to eliminate it.
keyPoints: Each thread updates histogram[thread_id], but if bins are contiguous, adjacent bins may share cache line
Thread 0 updating bin[0] and Thread 1 updating bin[1] causes invalidation ping-pong if same line
Solution 1: Pad each bin to 64 bytes: struct { int count; char pad[60]; } bins[N];
Solution 2: Use thread-local accumulation - each thread has private array, combine at end with reduction
Solution 3: Use OpenMP reduction clause which automatically handles this pattern
Analysis: Show how invalidation rate drops and performance improves with padding

questions.question: Design and analyze a parallel matrix-vector multiplication algorithm (y = Ax) in OpenMP that avoids false sharing. Consider row-wise and element-wise partitioning.
keyPoints: Row-wise: Each thread computes y[i] = sum(A[i][j] * x[j]) for a set of rows
Potential false sharing: If multiple threads write to adjacent y[i] elements sharing cache line
Solution: Use private accumulator variables for each thread's partial sums
At end, use reduction or combine private results into y array
Element-wise (column-wise): Each thread computes portion of dot product for single y[i] - may have more sharing
Cache considerations: Access to x is read-only (good), A has good spatial locality, y writes are the concern
Use schedule(static) to group writes to same thread, reducing cache line contention
Apply padding if necessary or use array of structures vs structure of arrays layout

questions.question: Write and compare two OpenMP programs that compute parallel array sum—one prone to false sharing and one optimized. Analyze the performance characteristics.
keyPoints: Performance: Show timing comparison, discuss scalability
Key insight: Even with reduction, very large N may still show effects if reduction implementation uses shared accumulators
